genetic_lineage.overview: Pinot Noir is one of the most genetically significant grape varieties, serving as a parent to over 20 known varieties including Chardonnay. It likely originated in Burgundy over 1,000 years ago.

parents.name: Unknown (Proto-Pinot)
parents.notes: Likely a single variety that spontaneously crossed or mutated. May be descended from wild Vitis sylvestris.

offspring.name: Chardonnay
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. World's most famous white variety.

offspring.name: Aligoté
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. Burgundy's second white variety.

offspring.name: Gamay
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. The grape of Beaujolais.

offspring.name: Auxerrois
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. Important in Alsace and Luxembourg.

offspring.name: Melon de Bourgogne
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. Grape of Muscadet.

offspring.name: Romorantin
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. Rare Loire variety.

offspring.name: Knipperlé
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. Rare Alsatian variety.

offspring.name: Roublot
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. Nearly extinct.

offspring.name: Peurion
offspring.notes: Cross with Gouais Blanc. Nearly extinct.

mutations.description: Pink/grey-skinned mutation discovered in Burgundy. Major variety in Alsace, Italy, and Oregon.
White-skinned mutation. Important in Alsace and Alto Adige.
Mutation with downy leaf undersides. Essential Champagne variety.
Red-fleshed mutation. Rare, used for adding color.
Early-ripening mutation found in German-speaking regions.

genetic_lineage.genetic_significance: Pinot Noir's prolific crossing with Gouais Blanc produced many of France's most important varieties. The variety's genetic instability leads to frequent mutations, explaining the numerous Pinot clones and color variants. Pinot Noir is arguably the most genetically significant grape variety in the world.
